name: Recover a compact correctness-safe 4-row PTX writer sweep

summary.name: Recover a compact correctness-safe 4-row PTX writer sweep
summary.hypothesis: The round-8 repair showed that simply appending TileRow 2 and 3 restores correctness but also restores the old 198-register anchor. The next meaningful variant is to keep the new per-tile export helper shape but make the full 4-row sweep more compact than the current explicit 0/1/2/3 expansion, for example through a tighter compile-time row walker or paired-row emission that still writes every row exactly once.
summary.expected_bottleneck: occupancy_latency_hiding_issue in the PTX export/store path if a compact full-row sweep can retain some of the low-register behavior while staying correct

score_breakdown.reason: Best next move because it directly tests whether the round-7 low-register signal can survive a better full-row writer shape.

ranking_notes: Round 7 is still worth mining because it doubled active warps and cut registers drastically.
Round 8 falsified only the naive correctness repair shape, not the overall value of a tighter writer.
Keep this bounded to the PTX export sweep; do not mix in barrier or geometry changes.
summary.stop_condition: Stop if a compact full-row sweep still snaps back to the same 198-register profile within noise, or if correctness fails again.

target_hotspots.explanation: The direct correctness repair returned to 198 registers, which is exactly the regression this direction tries to avoid.
The target is to recover at least part of the lost active-warps gain while staying correct.

expected_local_changes: Keep full 4-row correctness, but replace the current direct 0/1/2/3 expansion with a more compact row sweep.
Preserve the new per-tile export helper and late address arithmetic shape if possible.
Avoid mixing this with barrier retiming or geometry changes.

guardrail_metrics.reason: The compact writer is only meaningful if it stays correct.
A writer-side refinement should not get worse than the current correct anchor.

notes: Node C may now implement this one candidate.
